title: Foundational hand, lower-case alphabet
subtitle: The twenty-six letters built on the seven basic strokes
excerpt: The Foundational lower-case alphabet is Edward Johnston's synthesis of the Caroline minuscule: a circular body stroke, an upright stem, and a consistent 30° nib angle throughout. Once the seven basic strokes are established, the twenty-six letters follow from fewer than a dozen letterforms combined in sequence.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapters on the Foundational hand, the minuscule alphabet, and stroke order. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john. Johnston's plates for the Foundational lower-case are the canonical source; these letter shapes have been in continuous use in calligraphy education since 1906.

glossaryTerms.definition: The prescribed sequence and direction of strokes for writing a letter. Following the ductus produces the correct weight distribution and finished shape.
The height of the lower-case letters, measured in nib widths from baseline to x-height line. Foundational lower-case uses 4 nib widths.
The angle of the broad nib's edge to the baseline, in degrees. Foundational holds 30° throughout the lower-case.
The branching curve that connects the stem of letters like n, m, h to their second or third leg. In Foundational, the arch begins slightly above the mid-height of the stem and curves upward.

content.text: The Foundational lower-case is built from a single model letter: the lower-case o. Every letter in the alphabet relates to the o either in width, in arch construction, or in stem weight. The
nib angle
is 30° throughout. The
x-height

is 4 nib widths (a size 3 Mitchell at 4 nib widths gives a text-sized letter; a size 0 at 4 nib widths gives display-size letters). The letters group into families: o-family (o c e d g q), n-family (n m h b p r), i-family (i u j), and the wider letters (w, v, x, y, z).
What you need

items.name: Iron-gall ink or carbon-black ink
items.qty: Small amount in a low open jar

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, practice sheets with guide lines
items.qty: Multiple sheets (rule or photocopy guide lines from the ruling technique tutorial)

items.name: Tracing paper (as hand guard)
items.qty: 1 sheet placed under the writing hand

content.text: The model letter, o

The Foundational o is a circle, slightly compressed into an oval. It is written in two strokes: stroke 1 is the left arc (from the top of the letter, curving left and down to the baseline in a C shape); stroke 2 is the right arc (from the top, curving right and down to close the bottom of the letter). Both strokes start at approximately the 1-o'clock position. The
ductus
produces consistent stress: the thickest parts of the o are at the left and right sides at mid-height; the thinnest parts are at the top and bottom.
The o-family letters: c, e, d, g, q

The letters c and e are partial o-shapes. The c is the left arc of the o, leaving the right side open. The e adds a horizontal bar to the c, crossing at mid-height. Both d and q begin with the full o-circle, then add a stem: d rises to the ascender line (7 nib widths); q drops to the descender line (3 nib widths below the baseline). The g is an o plus a descender with a loop. Practice the o first, then each derivative letter.
The n-family letters: n, m, h, b, p, r
The n begins with a straight downstroke from x-height to baseline (stroke 1), then an
arch

from just below the top of the stem, curving upward and over to the right, then straight down to the baseline (stroke 2). The arch lifts from the stem at approximately 40% of the x-height from the baseline. The m adds a second arch at the same height. The h is an n with a stem that extends to the ascender line. The b extends the stem up; the p extends it down.
The i-family and remaining letters

The i is a straight downstroke plus a dot above the x-height line, placed centrally over the stem. The u is an n upside down. The j is an i whose stem descends to the descender line with a small hook to the left. The remaining letters (k, l, t, f, s, v, w, x, y, z) derive from combinations of the strokes already practised. Practise each family group for at least twenty minutes before moving to the next.
Spacing and rhythm

Consistent inter-letter spacing is as important as accurate letter shapes. In Foundational, the visual white space between adjacent letters should be approximately equal to the white space inside the lower-case o. Between two straight-sided letters (like in or ni), add slightly more space than between two curved letters (oo). The eye adjusts optically: trust the visual result more than the measured distance.
Care
Clean the nib after each session with lukewarm water. Do not leave iron-gall ink to dry in a nib: it corrodes the metal. Store nibs dry and loose in a small tin or card envelope.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems in writing the Foundational lower-case.

items.symptom: The o looks like an egg, one side is notably wider than the other
items.cause: The two strokes of the o are not symmetrical starting positions. Stroke 1 begins too far to the left or too far to the right.
items.fix: Both strokes start from the same point: roughly the 1-o'clock position on the o circle, not from directly above or directly to the side. Check the starting position before each stroke.

items.symptom: The arch in n letters bumps upward above the x-height line
items.cause: The arch is lifting too early, before the stroke has settled into the correct high point.
items.fix: The arch should not exceed the x-height line. Begin the arch pull from inside the stem at approximately 40% of the letter height from the baseline, well within the body of the letter.
